title: Slack Architecture

content.question: Where does your business have zero slack? What would happen if one key thing broke tomorrow?
content.equation: SA = (Reserve + Cash + Time + Talent + Supplier Redundancy) ÷ Operational Load
sop: List your critical dependencies, key supplier, key platform, key person, and rate how much backup each has.
Calculate your cash runway in days or weeks if revenue stopped tomorrow.
For your single point of least redundancy, identify one backup option (second supplier, cross-trained employee, backup platform) and start setting it up.
Rate Reserve, Cash, Time, and Talent 1-10 each, plus Supplier Redundancy 1-10, and rate Operational Load 1-10 (see calculation, page 5)
Re-check slack quarterly, growth quietly eats reserves if you don't watch it.
content.explainer: Slack looks wasteful until the moment you need it. Then it's the only thing that saves you.
questions: Where does your business have zero slack? What would happen if one key thing broke tomorrow?
How many days could you operate if your top supplier or platform disappeared without warning?
Which team member, if they got sick for two weeks, would stop something critical from happening?
content.equation_example: Rate Reserve, Cash, Time, Talent, and Supplier Redundancy 1-10 each, then rate Operational Load 1-10. Reserve 6, Cash 7, Time 5, Talent 6, Supplier Redundancy 4, sum 28, Operational Load 7 → SA = 28 ÷ 7 = 4. Higher SA means you have real buffer if something breaks; lower means you're running too tight to absorb a shock.
content.closing_statement: Build reserves before you need them, not after.

caption:
Principle Nº 29: Slack Architecture

Resilience requires spare capacity. Slack looks wasteful until the moment you need it.

Ask yourself: Where does your business have zero slack? What would happen if one key thing broke tomorrow?
